mechanism_description: Reduce the incineration of plastics to cut greenhouse gas emissions from waste combustion.
primary_causal_pathway: Waste management practice (↓ plastic incineration) → ↓ fossil-carbon combustion → ↓ direct CO₂e emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Waste system reduces the share/volume of plastics sent to incineration (e.g., through prevention, diversion, reuse, or recycling).  
(2) Mediating process: Less combustion of fossil-derived polymer carbon reduces stack CO₂ (and associated combustion-related gases) from waste-to-energy/incineration facilities.  
(3) Climate outcome: Lower total GHG emissions from the waste sector, contributing to mitigation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced direct CO₂ emissions by avoiding the combustion of fossil-based plastics in incinerators.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Avoiding plastic combustion reduces direct fossil CO₂ emissions from incineration.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: The primary intended and largest climate effect is emissions reduction (not primarily reducing material/energy input for the same service as an efficiency framing, and not adaptation or ecosystem-based outcomes).  
3) Edge cases applied: If reduced incineration shifts plastic to landfills, methane effects are typically minor for plastics but the net mitigation benefit could shrink; if it shifts to recycling/prevention, mitigation benefit increases (including upstream avoided production). Regardless, the mechanism’s main pathway remains combustion-emissions reduction.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy whose shared mechanism is “plastic incineration reduction → waste combustion emissions reduction” has mitigation as its dominant climate-relevant outcome.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether the policy is best evidenced as “waste diversion,” “circular economy,” “fuel switching,” or “facility operations” is policy-specific and should be assigned later based on implementation details.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the policy reduces incineration but results in substantial diversion to fossil-intensive alternative treatments (e.g., long-haul transport to other incinerators, or increased virgin plastic production due to poor substitution), net mitigation may differ; however, the canonical mechanism still centers on direct combustion-emissions reduction.
co_benefits: Reduced local air pollutants/toxic emissions from incineration; improved public health; reduced waste-sector pollution burdens.
instance_edge_case_notes: Instrument is underspecified (could alternatively be a regulatory ban/cap, pricing disincentive, or infrastructure investment). Classified as target-setting because the text states an outcome without naming a policy mechanism.
